critique.summary: The article presents an interactive web experience called 'Web Rewind' created by Opera to explore 30 years of the web from 1995 to 2025. It appears to be a timeline-based journey highlighting web history artifacts, but the provided content is mostly navigational and promotional text without substantive historical information.
mainPoints: Web Rewind is an interactive journey through 30 years of the web.
The timeline covers years from 1995 through 2025.
The experience is made by Opera and includes features like sound effects and voiceovers.
Users can submit memories for a chance to win a trip.
critique.truthfulness: The article does not contain specific factual claims about web history but rather describes an interactive experience created by Opera. Opera is a known web browser company, and it is plausible that they developed such a project. However, the content provided lacks detailed historical information or multiple sources to verify the accuracy of any historical claims.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed historical context or examples from the 30 years of web history.
No external sources or perspectives are cited to support the information.
The interactive experience may require visiting the actual website to assess its educational value and accuracy.
The promotional nature of the content suggests a marketing purpose rather than a comprehensive historical analysis.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion reflects nostalgia for the classic Opera browser and disappointment over its transition to Chromium and acquisition by a Chinese company. Some users appreciate the historical significance and unique features of old Opera, while others criticize the current state and marketing efforts around its 30th anniversary.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I am sure that there are reasons that they cannot easily do this, but I really wish that they'd open source their Presto browser engine now that they've moved to Chromium anyway. I always liked the way that classic Opera made web pages look. Maybe it's just rose tinted glasses but it felt like Opera had a nice smoothness to it, almost like a PDF or something.

If they FOSS'd their old engine, conceivably someone could modernize it and we'd at least have one more competitor in the browser space, though typing this out I'm realizing that maybe that's why they haven't opened it up in the first place.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the value of the Presto engine and the potential benefits of open sourcing it, reflecting a realistic understanding of browser development.

comments.author: spikej
comments.text:
Opera was my secret weapon back in the day: if it worked in Opera, it would be guaranteed to work in Chrome, IE and Firefox. It significantly reduced the browser quirks stuff I'd have to dig into.

Dragonfly was top notch also: one of the best bits was ability to outline all the elements on the page. There were other features too that weren't (still aren't) in the other browser dev tools
comments.analysis: The comment accurately recalls Opera's role in web development and its superior developer tools, providing a valuable perspective on its historical strengths.

comments.author: al_borland
comments.text:
I have fond memories of Opera. When I migrated off of it to Phoenix, I had a really hard time adjusting to not having mouse gestures. I didn’t know how anyone lived without them.

By the time extensions came around to mimic Opera’s mouse gestures on other browsers, I could never get used to actually using them again.

I was sad to see Opera become just another incarnation of Chrome.
comments.analysis: This personal experience highlights a key feature of Opera that was missed after switching browsers, reflecting common user sentiments.

comments.author: irusensei
comments.text: I remember trying Opera for the first time in Windows 98 SE. It was one of those versions that prided itself for fitting on a floppy. I think it was 3.0.6 or 3.6. But anyway I was taken by surprise how good it was in comparison to Internet Explorer which at the time was the only browser I ever used.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a credible historical anecdote about Opera's early versions and their efficiency compared to contemporaries.

comments.author: davej
comments.text: I remember using Opera on my Windows 95, 60mhz Pentium with 8mb RAM. I remember the persistent banner ad that was part of the browser UI. I had no problem putting up with the ad because it performed incredibly well compared to IE and Netscape on my hardware. If I remember correctly they were the first browser to support game changing web features like alpha transparency in PNG images.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately recalls Opera's pioneering support for web technologies, adding valuable context to its innovation history.

comments.author: netsharc
comments.text: Feels as soulless as the Opera that's been bought by a Chinese company to sell predatory lending: https://qz.com/africa/1788351/operas-okash-opesas-predatory-...
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a critical view of Opera's current ownership and business practices, supported by a linked article, though it is emotionally charged.

comments.author: superkuh
comments.text: Opera is not 30. Opera is dead. Opera died and never went beyond version 12.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common sentiment that the original Opera ended with version 12, and the current browser is a different product, which is factually accurate.

comments.author: f-serif
comments.text:
Wow, this is pure gold. I skipped first time thinking it was just random page viewers from past.

This is impressive design, presentation and experience.

Thank you for the experience.
comments.analysis: The comment positively appreciates the design and experience of the web rewind project, showing enthusiasm but limited technical insight.

comments.author: ivankra
comments.text:
Eh, marketing fluff. This is more like it: https://oldweb.today/ - browse old web (from archive.org) with old browsers (in Wasm)

A better way to celebrate 30 years of their browser would be to just open source it. Code's been leaked and irrelevant today anyway but still.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a constructive alternative to the project and suggests open sourcing Opera as a meaningful tribute, reflecting a practical viewpoint.

comments.author: jlarocco
comments.text:
Sorry, but what this is supposed to be. It's just a spinning WebGL model?

I wish they would rewind back to using Presto and being an independent Norwegian company, but I'm sure everybody who made it a great browser back then is long gone.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses disappointment with the project and nostalgia for the original Opera, though it lacks constructive suggestions.
